basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Qalam — “the Pen” — opens with an enigmatic oath: “Nūn. By the pen and by what they inscribe!” followed by a consolation to the Prophet accused of madness: “You are not possessed… you are of a sublime character.” Qushayrī meditates at length on this khuluq ʿaẓīm: in him are gathered the scattered virtues of the prophets; offered the keys of the earth, he refused them; raised in the Ascension and shown all the Kingdom, he did not stop at it — “the sight neither swerved nor overstepped.” And on the reward “without measure” (ajr ghayr mamnūn): when your aspiration rose above the seeking of compensations, God established the reward for you; you want no wage — and that is why you are not, like your peers, a captive of compensations. The sūra unfolds the portrait of the slanderer, the parable of the people of the garden who swore to harvest at dawn “without saying: if God wills,” the warning against istidrāj — the imperceptible drawing-in through blessings while forgetting the Giver — and closes on the example of Jonah, “the man of the fish,” and the patience the heart needs under the decree of its Lord.

versets.trad: Nūn. By the pen and by what they inscribe!
versets.commentaire: Nūn (ن): an isolated letter for which Qushayrī reports several readings — the fish on whose back the world is said to rest, or the inkwell, or the first letter of Naṣīr (the Helper) and Nūr (the Light). wa-l-qalam: by the pen, the instrument of writing. God swears by the pen and by what it traces: knowledge fixed, transmitted, lasting. At the threshold of a sūra defending the Prophet against the charge of madness, this oath by the pen is eloquent: the Word is no raving of a madman but a graven, weighed, ordered writing.

versets.trad: By the grace of your Lord, you are not possessed!
versets.commentaire: mā anta bi-niʿmati rabbika bi-majnūn (ما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنون): by the grace of your Lord, you are not mad. Qushayrī: “what the enemies said of him — that he was possessed — cast dread into his breast; God removes it by this denial, and confirms it by the preceding oath. This is God's way with His messenger: what the enemies say of him, God turns back upon them and removes from him.” Slander wounds, but God Himself takes up the defence of His beloved. The comfort comes from on high: it is by the grace of your Lord that the accusation falls.

mots.racine: ن ع م / ر ب ب
mots.sens: by the grace of your Lord

versets.trad: For you there is a reward without end,
versets.commentaire: ajran ghayra mamnūn (أَجْرًا غَيْرَ مَمْنون): an endless, undiminished reward. A pearl of Qushayrī: “when his aspiration rose above the seeking of compensations (al-aʿwāḍ), God established the reward for him, saying: you have an undiminished reward — though you do not want it. And of this great reward is this very character: you do not want the wage — and by Us you want nothing; had We not singled you out with this freedom, you would be like your peers, captives of compensations.” The highest wage is to seek no wage: the freedom of the heart that acts for God alone.

versets.trad: and you are of a sublime character.
versets.commentaire: khuluq ʿaẓīm (خُلُق عَظيم): a sublime character. Qushayrī unfolds: “as God made known to him the accounts of the prophets before him, He made him know that their scattered virtues are gathered in him.” And he gives the proofs: he was offered the keys of the earth and refused them; raised in the Ascension and shown all the Kingdom and the Garden, he did not stop at it — “the sight neither swerved nor overstepped.” The sublime character is also: “neither does affliction divert him, nor does the gift distract him”; wounded in the face, he said “O God, forgive my people, they do not know”; when each will say “myself, myself,” he will say “my community, my community.” Gabriel taught him virtue: “join with the one who cuts you off, give to the one who deprives you, forgive the one who wrongs you.”

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ع ظ م
mots.def: khuluq: character, moral nature; ʿaẓīm: immense.

versets.trad: Soon you will see, and they too will see
versets.commentaire: fa-sa-tubṣiru wa-yubṣirūn (فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَيُبْصِرون): soon you will see and they will see. Time will settle what slander confuses: who was in the light and who in error. The Prophet need not defend himself by dispute; it is enough to wait for the truth to unveil itself. To the charge of madness answers this calm assurance: reality will show itself, and the verdict of hasty men will be overturned by the verdict of the facts.

versets.trad: which of you is the afflicted one.
versets.commentaire: al-maftūn (المَفْتون): Qushayrī glosses: “the possessed, for he was afflicted (futin) with madness.” The irony of the verse: it is the accusers who will discover which of them is really the strayed, the “possessed.” The accusation turns back: the one who calls another mad often reveals his own madness. On the Day, or as soon as the truth unveils itself, it will be known on which side reason stood and on which blindness. The slanderer judges the other and judges himself.

versets.ar: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ
versets.tr: inna rabbaka huwa aʿlamu bi-man ḍalla ʿan sabīlihi wa-huwa aʿlamu bi-l-muhtadīna
versets.trad: Truly, your Lord knows best who has strayed from His path, and He knows best the well-guided.
versets.commentaire: aʿlamu bi-man ḍalla… wa… bi-l-muhtadīn (أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ وَبِالمُهْتَدين): He knows best the strayed and the well-guided. The debate over “who is mad” is left to its sole Judge: God knows, better than anyone, who has strayed and who is guided. The servant need not settle what God alone sees; it is enough to walk straight and leave the judgement to the One who probes hearts. This trust delivers from obsession with others' regard: what matters is not men's labels but God's knowledge.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م
ض ل ل / س ب ل
mots.sens: who has strayed from His path

versets.trad: So do not obey the deniers.
versets.commentaire: fa-lā tuṭiʿi l-mukadhdhibīn (فَلا تُطِعِ المُكَذِّبين): do not obey the deniers. Qushayrī draws a maxim: “your Adored One is one, let your aim be one; and when you witness one aim, let your witnessed be one.” The Oneness of God calls for the oneness of the heart: not to divide oneself, not to yield for the sake of pleasing. Firmness is not harshness but fidelity to the One. To obey the deniers would be to fragment one's orientation; the heart turned to God alone is not swayed by those who deny.

versets.trad: They would like you to compromise, so they would compromise.
versets.commentaire: waddū law tudhinu fa-yudhinūn (وَدّوا لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنون): they would like you to soften, then they would soften. Idhān is compromise on the truth, the oil one applies to smooth things over. Qushayrī: “whoever wakes ill wishes all people to be ill; likewise the one branded with the iron of separation wishes his enemy to share it.” The deniers want to draw the Prophet into their lukewarmness: let him yield a little, and they would yield a little. But truth is not negotiated by halves. To refuse compromise is no rigidity but the safeguarding of the message's integrity.

versets.trad: And do not obey the contemptible swearer of oaths,
versets.commentaire: ḥallāf mahīn (حَلّاف مَهين): a great swearer of oaths, contemptible. The beginning of a portrait in successive strokes (v. 10-13) of the human type one must not follow. Qushayrī gives each trait a spiritual echo: the swearer, “he is the one fallen from Our regard, whom We have abased by distancing him from Us.” To swear constantly betrays the lack of weight in one's word: the truthful need no oaths. The contempt (mahīn) is no insult but a statement: the one who has debased himself by lying has lost his worth in God's eyes.

mots.racine: ح ل ف / م ه ن
mots.def: ḥallāf: who swears much; mahīn: base.

versets.trad: the defamer who spreads slander,
versets.commentaire: hammāz mashshāʾ bi-namīm (هَمّاز مَشّاء بِنَميم): backbiter, tale-bearer. Qushayrī: “veiled from Us, chastised by abandonment, for attacking Our saints.” Two joined vices: backbiting (hamz) that wounds in absence, and tale-bearing (namīma) that sows discord from one to another. The one who spends his time disparaging others cuts himself off from God: the tongue that wounds the servants draws away from the Lord. Slander does not strike only its target; it first damns the one who utters it.

mots.racine: م ش ي / ن م م

versets.trad: the hinderer of good, the transgressor, the inveterate sinner,
versets.commentaire: mannāʿ li-l-khayr (مَنّاع لِلْخَيْر): who hinders the good. Qushayrī: “abased by avarice (shuḥḥ), stripped of divine assistance; a transgressor (muʿtad), deprived of modesty, scattered in the valleys of deprivation.” The portrait deepens: not only does he slander, but he obstructs the good, transgresses the bounds, sinks into sin. Avarice is the heart of it all: to withhold the good one could spread. The one who hinders others' good deprives himself of his own, and finds himself “scattered” far from the heart's unity.

mots.racine: م ن ع / خ ي ر
ع د و / أ ث م
mots.def: muʿtad: who exceeds bounds; athīm: sinner.

versets.trad: coarse and, on top of that, ignoble.
versets.commentaire: ʿutull… zanīm (عُتُلّ زَنيم): coarse, and on top of that base in origin. Qushayrī: “of low stock, devoid of merit, dogged in his quarrel by his falsehood, never returning to any good within him.” The last stroke of the portrait: brutal coarseness (ʿutull) and baseness (zanīm). The whole draws the finished type of the corrupt — slanderer, miser, transgressor, brutal. And the sequel (v. 14) gives its root: all this because he “has wealth and sons.” Wealth, ill-used, swells these vices instead of correcting them.

mots.sens: and on top of that ignoble in origin

versets.trad: Do not obey him merely because he abounds in wealth and sons.
versets.commentaire: an kāna dhā mālin wa-banīn (أَنْ كانَ ذا مالٍ وَبَنين): because he has wealth and sons. Qushayrī connects: “do not obey him — despite these vices and flaws — for his fortune, his portion of this world, and the number of his children.” Here is the trap: wealth and offspring impress and silence. One would be tempted to follow the well-endowed man, even if corrupt, for his outward glitter. The verse warns: do not be dazzled by the wealth and sons of a perverted heart. It is not the world's advantages that make a man's worth.

mots.racine: م و ل / ب ن ي

versets.ar: إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ آيَاتُنَا قَالَ أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: idhā tutlā ʿalayhi āyātunā qāla asāṭīru l-awwalīna
versets.trad: When Our verses are recited to him, he says: “Fables of the ancients!”
versets.commentaire: asāṭīru l-awwalīn (أَساطيرُ الأَوَّلين): fables of the ancients. Qushayrī: “he receives them by denial and judges the Quran to be legend.” Here is the verdict of the enriched corrupt one (v. 10-14): to reduce the Word to old tales. It is the defence of sated pride: if it is only a fable, I need not submit to it. To label “legend” what disturbs excuses one from listening. But this contempt does not touch the Word; it reveals only the closed heart of the one who utters it.

mots.racine: ت ل و / أ ي ي
mots.sens: when Our verses are recited to him
mots.def: talā: to recite; āya: sign, verse.

mots.racine: س ط ر / أ و ل
mots.sens: he says: fables of the ancients

versets.trad: We shall brand him on the snout!
versets.commentaire: sa-nasimuhu ʿalā l-khurṭūm (سَنَسِمُهُ عَلى الخُرْطوم): We shall brand him on the snout. Qushayrī: “We shall make for him on the Day, on his nose, a mark that will disfigure him, so that he may be known by it.” The word khurṭūm (snout, trunk) is itself debasing: the proud nose, lifted to despise, will be branded with infamy. The sanction espouses the fault: the organ of haughtiness becomes the place of shame. The one who turned up his nose at the Word will see it disfigured. Pride, which sought to rise, is brought down to a visible mark.

mots.def: khurṭūm: snout, trunk (debasing for the nose).

versets.ar: إِنَّا بَلَوْنَاهُمْ كَمَا بَلَوْنَا أَصْحَابَ الْجَنَّةِ إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا لَيَصْرِمُنَّهَا مُصْبِحِينَ
versets.tr: innā balawnāhum kamā balawnā aṣḥāba l-jannati idh aqsamū la-yaṣrimunnahā muṣbiḥīna
versets.trad: Truly, We tested them as We tested the owners of the garden, when they swore to harvest it at morning;
versets.commentaire: aṣḥāb al-janna (أَصْحاب الجَنّة): the people of the garden. The start of a major parable (v. 17-33). Qushayrī reports the story: a pious man left to the poor all that the scythe overshot, all that fell from the cloth during the harvest; at his death, his heirs judged that this giving scattered their wealth, and swore to harvest everything at dawn, before the poor arrived, and to leave them nothing. The parable forewarns the Meccans: you will be tested as they were. The garden blessed by the father's generosity is to be ruined by the sons' avarice. The test reveals the heart: what one does with the gift received decides everything.

mots.racine: ب ل و / ج ن ن
mots.sens: as We tested the people of the garden
mots.def: aṣḥāb al-janna: the owners of the garden.

mots.racine: ق س م / ص ر م / ص ب ح
mots.sens: when they swore to harvest it at morning
mots.def: ṣarama: to harvest; muṣbiḥ: at morning.

versets.trad: but they swore without making any reservation.
versets.commentaire: wa-lā yastathnūn (وَلا يَسْتَثْنون): without making a reservation. The decisive fault is here, seemingly trivial: they swore without saying “if God wills” (in shā' Allāh). Qushayrī, later, reads this reservation as the heart of tasbīḥ — the acknowledgement that nothing happens except by the will of God. To omit “if God wills” is to think oneself master of tomorrow, to forget that the future is in God's hands. This quiet presumption — to dispose of the morrow as of a due — is the root of their ruin. The little word forgotten betrays a great forgetting: that of dependence upon the Lord.

mots.def: istathnā: to except, reserve (to say ‘if God wills’).

versets.ar: فَطَافَ عَلَيْهَا طَائِفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ وَهُمْ نَائِمُونَ
versets.tr: fa-ṭāfa ʿalayhā ṭāʾifun min rabbika wa-hum nāʾimūna
versets.trad: A visitation from your Lord swept over it while they slept
versets.commentaire: ṭāfa ʿalayhā ṭāʾif min rabbik (طافَ عَلَيْها طائِفٌ مِنْ رَبِّك): a scourge from your Lord passed over it. Qushayrī: “He sent down from heaven a scourge that burned their fruit.” While they slept, sure of their harvest for the morrow, the decree was carried out in silence. An image of the precariousness of all human calculation: one sleeps on one's certainties, and the night undoes what the day foresaw. The sleep of the owners speaks of their heedlessness; the scourge that visits unknown to them speaks of God's sovereignty over what they thought they held.

mots.def: ṭāʾif: a scourge that comes around.

versets.trad: and by morning it was as if all had been harvested.
versets.commentaire: ka-l-ṣarīm (كَالصَّريم): like the black night, or like an already-reaped field. Qushayrī: “like the darkened night.” By morning, the verdant garden is but a black, devastated expanse, as if scorched. The contrast is cruel: they expected an abundant harvest, they find ruin. What was to be their wealth has become darkness. The devastated garden is the mirror of the heart that, having withheld the good (the avarice of v. 12), finds itself stripped of all. One loses what one wanted to keep for oneself alone.

mots.sens: like the black night / a reaped field
mots.def: ṣarīm: dark night, or finished harvest.

versets.trad: At dawn they called out to one another:
versets.commentaire: fa-tanādaw muṣbiḥīn (فَتَنادَوْا مُصْبِحين): they called to one another at morning. Still unaware of the disaster, they hail one another to set out early for the harvest. The irony is suspended: they organize with zeal for a harvest that no longer exists. Their very eagerness underlines their blindness. The morning that was to be one of abundance will be one of bitter discovery. How many human efforts are deployed thus upon certainties already ruined unbeknownst to us.

mots.sens: they called out to one another

versets.ar: أَنِ اغْدُوا عَلَىٰ حَرْثِكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَارِمِينَ
versets.tr: ani ghdū ʿalā ḥarthikum in kuntum ṣārimīna
versets.trad: “Set out early to your field if you would harvest.”
versets.commentaire: ghdū ʿalā ḥarthikum (اغْدوا عَلى حَرْثِكُمْ): go early to your field. They hasten toward their harvest before the poor awake. The haste (al-ghadw, setting out at dawn) betrays their intention: to outstrip the needy so as to share nothing. This rush toward profit, in contempt of the poor's right, is the exact reverse of the father's generosity. They run toward what they think is their wealth, not knowing they run toward their ruin. The miser's haste hurries him toward the void.

mots.racine: غ د و / ح ر ث
mots.sens: set out early to your field
mots.def: ghadā: to set out at morning; ḥarth: cultivated field.

versets.trad: They set off, speaking to one another in low tones:
versets.commentaire: yatakhāfatūn (يَتَخافَتون): they whisper. Qushayrī: “they lowered their voices among themselves so that none would hear.” The whisper betrays the bad conscience: one murmurs what one would not dare say aloud. Their plot to deprive the poor is woven in secret, like all that fears the light. Discretion here is not modesty but concealment: they know deep down that their design is shameful, and they hide it. What is done in low tones against another's right already carries its own condemnation.

versets.ar: أَن لَّا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُم مِّسْكِينٌ
versets.tr: an lā yadkhulannahā l-yawma ʿalaykum miskīnun
versets.trad: “Let no poor man enter it today against your will!”
versets.commentaire: an lā yadkhulannahā… miskīn (أَنْ لا يَدْخُلَنَّها مِسْكين): let no poor man enter it. Here is the heart of their fault, said at last among themselves: to bar the entrance to the poor. The contrast with the father is total: he left the poor all the surplus; they shut the door. To exclude the needy from the gift is to break the bond that wealth was meant to tie. And it is precisely this exclusion that calls forth the ruin: in wanting to keep everything, they lose everything. The scorched garden is the exact answer to the door shut on the poor.

mots.sens: a poor man, against your will

versets.trad: They set out early, thus resolved.
versets.commentaire: ʿalā ḥardin qādirīn (عَلى حَرْدٍ قادِرين): resolved, thinking themselves able. Qushayrī gives two senses to ḥard: “able in their own eyes,” or “in anger against the poor.” The two meet: the presumption of power (“we are masters of our harvest”) and hardness of heart (the resentment toward the needy). They set out sure of themselves, forgetting the “if God wills” (v. 18). This confidence in their own power alone, joined to the refusal to share, is exactly what the scourge will belie. To think oneself able without God is to walk toward one's own impotence.

mots.racine: ح ر د / ق د ر
mots.def: ḥard: resolve, or anger; qādir: able.

versets.trad: When they saw what had happened, they said: “We have surely lost our way!
versets.commentaire: innā la-ḍāllūn (إنّا لَضالّون): we have lost our way. The first movement before the devastated garden: denial of the evidence. Qushayrī: “this is not our garden!” — they think they have taken the wrong path, so total is the ruin. It is the reflex of the stricken heart: to refuse at first to see. Then comes acknowledgement (v. 27). The “we have lost our way” first speaks of confusion, before becoming confession. Before the loss of what one wanted to keep for oneself, the first movement is incredulity, the second lucidity.

mots.sens: they said: we have lost our way

versets.trad: No, rather we are deprived!”
versets.commentaire: bal naḥnu maḥrūmūn (بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرومون): no, we are deprived. Denial gives way to lucidity: it is not that they took the wrong garden, but that they have been deprived of its harvest. Maḥrūm — deprived: the word states the just requital. They who wanted to deprive the poor (v. 24) find themselves deprived in turn. The deprivation inflicted turns into deprivation suffered: one reaps what one has sown. To acknowledge “we are deprived” is to begin to see the link between the fault and the loss.

versets.ar: قَالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ لَوْلَا تُسَبِّحُونَ
versets.tr: qāla awsaṭuhum a-lam aqul lakum lawlā tusabbiḥūna
versets.trad: The most moderate among them said: “Did I not warn you? If only you had glorified God!”
versets.commentaire: awsaṭuhum (أَوْسَطُهُمْ): the most balanced, the most just among them. Qushayrī: “the most upright in conduct, the most beautiful in speech.” And lawlā tusabbiḥūn — “if only you glorified” — he reads thus: “if only you had made the reservation, saying: if God wills.” The tasbīḥ (glorification) here meets the “if God wills” of verse 18: to acknowledge that nothing happens without God's will is already to glorify Him, to declare Him transcendent and master of all. The fault was not only avarice, but the forgetting of this dependence. The most just one had seen rightly: the glorification of God would have changed their heart, and perhaps their fate.

mots.def: awsaṭ: the most just, the most balanced.
sabbaḥa: to glorify (and say ‘if God wills’).

versets.ar: قَالُوا سُبْحَانَ رَبِّنَا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَالِمِينَ
versets.tr: qālū subḥāna rabbinā innā kunnā ẓālimīna
versets.trad: They said: “Glory to our Lord! Truly, we were wrongdoers”;
versets.commentaire: subḥāna rabbinā innā kunnā ẓālimīn (سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا إنّا كُنّا ظالِمين): glory to our Lord, we were unjust. At last they glorify — but after the ruin. The glorification omitted at the start (v. 18) now bursts forth, joined to the confession of injustice. Misfortune has opened their eyes. Ẓālimīn — unjust: they acknowledge that their wrong was to deprive the poor and to think themselves masters of tomorrow. The confession is sincere, but the garden is lost. The glorification comes rightly — but late: had it come first, it might have saved the harvest. Better to glorify God before the ordeal than after.

mots.racine: س ب ح / ر ب ب
mots.sens: they said: glory to our Lord

versets.trad: and they turned to one another, exchanging reproaches.
versets.commentaire: yatalāwamūn (يَتَلاوَمون): they blame one another. After the collective confession, discord: each casts the common fault upon the other. The plot that united them in avarice (v. 23) comes apart in mutual accusations. Such is the bitter fruit of shared fault: it binds for a time then divides. The good gathers, evil scatters. Mutual reproaches mend nothing; they only add division to loss. Only the conversion of the heart, not the accusation of others, opens a way out.

versets.ar: قَالُوا يَا وَيْلَنَا إِنَّا كُنَّا طَاغِينَ
versets.tr: qālū yā waylanā innā kunnā ṭāghīna
versets.trad: “Woe to us,” they said, “we were rebellious!
versets.commentaire: innā kunnā ṭāghīn (إنّا كُنّا طاغين): we were rebels. The confession deepens: not only unjust (v. 29), but ṭāghīn — having overstepped all measure. Ṭughyān (the same root as for Pharaoh) is the root-evil: to cross the limit, to take oneself for master. In thinking themselves absolute owners of their harvest, in excluding the poor, they had overstepped the bounds. The lament “woe to us” accompanies this acknowledgement of excess. To acknowledge that one has overstepped is deeper than to acknowledge a mere fault: it touches the root.

versets.ar: عَسَىٰ رَبُّنَا أَن يُبْدِلَنَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا إِنَّا إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا رَاغِبُونَ
versets.tr: ʿasā rabbunā an yubdilanā khayran minhā innā ilā rabbinā rāghibūna
versets.trad: Perhaps our Lord will give us in exchange something better than this. To our Lord we turn in longing!”
versets.commentaire: innā ilā rabbinā rāghibūn (إنّا إلى رَبِّنا راغِبون): we turn, longing, toward our Lord. A luminous turning-point of the parable: after the confession, hope. Far from sinking into despair, they hope for a better exchange and turn to God with longing (raghba). It is the very movement of true repentance: to acknowledge one's fault, then to turn to God full of hope. The loss of the material garden opens the way to a higher good — the conversion of the heart. Sometimes the ruin of an earthly good is the beginning of a return to God more precious than any garden.

mots.sens: perhaps our Lord will exchange for us

versets.ar: كَذَٰلِكَ الْعَذَابُ وَلَعَذَابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: kadhālika l-ʿadhābu wa-la-ʿadhābu l-ākhirati akbaru law kānū yaʿlamūna
versets.trad: Such was the chastisement! But the chastisement of the Hereafter is greater, if only they knew!
versets.commentaire: kadhālika l-ʿadhāb (كَذلِكَ العَذاب): such is the chastisement. Qushayrī draws here a major teaching for the wayfarers (murīdūn), to be pondered: “such is the state of one who has had a beautiful beginning and receives divine assistance continuously, avoiding disobediences; God then grants him vigour, and the spiritual states shine in his inmost. But if he lets slip a false claim, or abandons an adab of service, those states close upon him; he falls back into the dryness of works. And if he neglects the obligations, his state is overturned: he is sent back from union to distancing, from nearness to exile far from the Door; his purity becomes hardness.” The lesson is in the endings (al-ʿibra bi-l-khawātīm). But Qushayrī adds hope: “it is not excluded that the Real should still look at him with His grace and welcome him, in regard to his beginnings — for God is tender toward His servants.”

mots.racine: أ خ ر / ك ب ر
mots.sens: the chastisement of the Hereafter is greater

versets.ar: إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ
versets.tr: inna li-l-muttaqīna ʿinda rabbihim jannāti l-naʿīmi
versets.trad: For the God-fearing there are, with their Lord, Gardens of bliss.
versets.commentaire: jannāti l-naʿīm (جَنّاتِ النَّعيم): the Gardens of bliss. To the earthly garden devastated by avarice (v. 17-33) answer the eternal Gardens granted to the God-fearing. Qushayrī: “those who guard against associationism and denial, then against disobediences, have with God the reward.” The contrast is intended: those who withheld the good lost their garden; those who feared God receive gardens that do not wither. The true garden is not the one jealously kept, but the one God gives to whoever fears Him and shares.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن ع م

versets.trad: Shall We treat those who submit to God like the guilty?
versets.commentaire: a-fa-najʿalu l-muslimīna ka-l-mujrimīn (أَفَنَجْعَلُ المُسْلِمين كَالمُجْرِمين): shall We make the submitters the equal of the criminals? A question whose answer is evident: no, God does not confuse the one who submits and the one who breaks the bond. Divine justice distinguishes. It would be absurd for the God-fearing and the corrupt to share the same fate. This verse answers every temptation to think it comes to the same thing — to live in submission or in crime. No: the two ways lead to two destinies. The distinction of ends attests God's justice and gives meaning to the present choice.

mots.racine: س ل م / ج ر م

versets.trad: What is the matter with you? How do you judge?
versets.commentaire: mā lakum kayfa taḥkumūn (ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمون): what is it with you, how do you judge? Qushayrī: “how do you judge? Have you a proof?” An apostrophe to those who would claim, in the Hereafter, the same fate as the believers, or better. On what do they base such a judgement? The series of questions (v. 36-41) dismantles this baseless claim. To judge that all is equal, or that one will have the best without having merited it, is to judge with no basis at all. The claim of the ego collapses as soon as it is asked for its proof.

mots.sens: what is the matter with you

versets.trad: Do you have a Book in which you study
versets.commentaire: am lakum kitābun fīhi tadrusūn (أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتابٌ فيهِ تَدْرُسون): do you have a Book in which you study? The first question of the series: do you have a revelation that would guarantee your claim? Of course not. Qushayrī: “the aim of these questions is to deny that.” One does not found a hope of salvation on the void. The claim of the ego — “I shall have the best unconditionally” — has neither Book, nor oath, nor guarantor. It is but a desire disguised as certainty. Only the revealed Word founds a true hope.

mots.sens: or do you have a Book

versets.trad: that you will have therein whatever you choose?
versets.commentaire: la-mā takhayyarūn (لَما تَخَيَّرون): what you would choose, what you covet. The claim unveiled: to believe one will have, in the Hereafter, exactly what one desires, as if heaven must bend to our preferences. It is to confuse desire with right. Would there be a Book guaranteeing that each obtains what he has chosen? No. The Hereafter is not a catalogue from which one picks at will; it is the just requital of what one has been. To take one's cravings for promises is the very illusion the sūra dismantles.

versets.ar: أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمَانٌ عَلَيْنَا بَالِغَةٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَمَا تَحْكُمُونَ
versets.tr: am lakum aymānun ʿalaynā bālighatun ilā yawmi l-qiyāmati inna lakum la-mā taḥkumūna
versets.trad: Or have you received from Us oaths binding until the Day of Resurrection, that you shall have whatever you decree?
versets.commentaire: aymānun ʿalaynā bāligha (أَيْمانٌ عَلَيْنا بالِغَة): binding oaths from Us. The second question: would God have committed Himself to them by a solemn pact, valid until the Day, guaranteeing all they decree? Obviously not. Qushayrī: “the aim is to deny that.” None holds from God a blank cheque. The claim is fully dismantled: neither Book (v. 37) nor oath (v. 39) founds the idea that one would obtain everything according to one's whim. God is bound by no engagement to grant our decrees: He is free, and just.

mots.sens: have you oaths binding upon Us

mots.racine: ب ل غ / ق و م
mots.sens: valid until the Day of Resurrection
that you shall have what you decree

versets.trad: Ask them which of them stands as guarantor for that.
versets.commentaire: ayyuhum bi-dhālika zaʿīm (أَيُّهُمْ بِذلِكَ زَعيم): which of them stands as guarantor for it? The challenge is thrown down: let a single one stand surety for their claim. None can. Zaʿīm — guarantor, respondent: who will take upon himself to guarantee that they will obtain all they want? No one. The claim remains without respondent, suspended in the void. To ask “who stands guarantor?” suffices to silence it: what has no guarantor has no foundation. Before the silence, the illusion dissolves.

mots.sens: which of them stands guarantor for it

versets.ar: أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَاءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا بِشُرَكَائِهِمْ إِن كَانُوا صَادِقِينَ
versets.tr: am lahum shurakāʾu fa-l-yaʾtū bi-shurakāʾihim in kānū ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: Do they have associates? Then let them bring their associates, if they are truthful.
versets.commentaire: fa-l-yaʾtū bi-shurakāʾihim (فَلْيَأْتوا بِشُرَكائِهِمْ): let them produce their associates. The last challenge: if they claim that other deities guarantee their fate, let them bring them forth. Of course, they cannot. The idolatrous claim is summoned to produce its proofs, and remains mute. In kānū ṣādiqīn — if they are truthful: the condition underlines the imposture. The whole series of challenges (v. 36-41) ends in the same conclusion: neither Book, nor oath, nor guarantor, nor associate. The claim of the ego and of idolatry is entirely empty. God alone is the true respondent.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ
versets.tr: yawma yukshafu ʿan sāqin wa-yudʿawna ilā l-sujūdi fa-lā yastaṭīʿūna
versets.trad: The Day when the shin is laid bare, they will be called to prostrate, but they will be unable.
versets.commentaire: yukshafu ʿan sāq (يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساق): lit. “the shin will be laid bare” — an expression of the extreme rigour of the Day, or, says Qushayrī, “the leg of the Throne.” They are then called to prostrate, fa-lā yastaṭīʿūn — and they cannot: “the believers prostrate, but the deniers' backs are stiffened, no longer bending.” A striking image: the one who did not wish to bow in life can no longer do so on the Day. Prostration refused out of pride becomes impossible by chastisement. The back that could not bend before God stays stiff when it must. One keeps, on the Day, the posture one forged in life.

mots.racine: ك ش ف / س و ق
mots.sens: the day the shin is laid bare

mots.racine: د ع و / س ج د

versets.ar: خَاشِعَةً أَبْصَارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ وَقَدْ كَانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سَالِمُونَ
versets.tr: khāshiʿatan abṣāruhum tarhaquhum dhillatun wa-qad kānū yudʿawna ilā l-sujūdi wa-hum sālimūna
versets.trad: Eyes downcast, covered with humiliation, for they had been called to prostrate while they were yet sound.
themes: the call in the time of health
versets.commentaire: wa-qad kānū yudʿawna ilā l-sujūdi wa-hum sālimūn (وَقَدْ كانوا يُدْعَوْنَ إلى السُّجودِ وَهُمْ سالِمون): they were called to prostrate while they were sound. Qushayrī: “this is recalled to them to increase their regret, and so that the argument against them be more crushing.” The contrast is cruel: in life, sound and free, they could prostrate and would not; on the Day, they would and can no longer. Sālimūn — sound: that was the time of grace, the time when prostration was possible and easy. To have refused it then makes the present inability all the more bitter. The time of health is the time of worship; to neglect it is to prepare a regret without remedy.

mots.racine: خ ش ع / ب ص ر
ر ه ق / ذ ل ل
mots.def: rahiqa: to cover, overwhelm; dhilla: humiliation.

versets.ar: فَذَرْنِي وَمَن يُكَذِّبُ بِهَٰذَا الْحَدِيثِ سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: fa-dharnī wa-man yukadhdhibu bi-hādhā l-ḥadīthi sa-nastadrijuhum min ḥaythu lā yaʿlamūna
versets.trad: So leave Me with those who deny this discourse; We shall lead them on by degrees from where they do not know.
versets.commentaire: sa-nastadrijuhum min ḥaythu lā yaʿlamūn (سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمون): We shall lead them on by degrees without their knowing. Qushayrī gives several definitions of istidrāj, fearsome and subtle: “to be heaped with blessings while forgetting gratitude”; “every time they increase in disobedience, He increases them in blessing”; “not to be chastised on the spot, the chastisement being deferred”; “occupation with the blessing while forgetting the Benefactor”; “to be deceived by the length of the respite”; “an enviable outside and a disordered inside.” Istidrāj is the trap of the affluent: to believe that abundance proves God's pleasure, when it may be a deceptive reprieve. The true danger is not lack, but the blessing that lulls.

mots.racine: و ذ ر / ك ذ ب / ح د ث
mots.sens: leave Me with the one who denies this discourse
mots.def: wadhara: to leave; ḥadīth: word, discourse (the Quran).

mots.sens: We shall lead them on by degrees
mots.def: istadraja: to draw on imperceptibly, by stages.

mots.sens: from where they do not know

versets.trad: I grant them respite, for My design is firm.
versets.commentaire: umlī lahum inna kaydī matīn (أُمْلي لَهُمْ إنَّ كَيْدي مَتين): I grant them respite, My design is firm. Qushayrī: “I let them go on; then, when I seize them, My seizing is painful and severe.” The respite (imlāʾ) is no forgetting nor weakness: it is God's patience that lets things ripen. The divine kayd — His unfathomable design — is “firm”: nothing escapes it. At the heart of the danger of istidrāj lies this truth: the respite granted may be a grace for the one who turns back, or a rope let out for the one who persists. The respite is a question posed: what will you do with it?

mots.racine: ك ي د / م ت ن

versets.ar: أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ أَجْرًا فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ
versets.tr: am tasʾaluhum ajran fa-hum min maghramin muthqalūna
versets.trad: Or do you ask them a wage, so that they are weighed down with debt?
versets.commentaire: am tasʾaluhum ajran (أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ أَجْرًا): do you ask them a wage? Qushayrī: “you ask no burden of them in exchange for your call, no tribute if they follow you; why then do they delay, why refuse to respond?” The Prophet's call is free: it costs nothing to those who heed it. Their refusal therefore has no material excuse. The gratuity of the call removes all pretext: they cannot say the way is too costly. What holds them back is not an imposed burden, but their own closed heart. The gift offered without return unmasks the refusal as pure obstinacy.

mots.racine: س أ ل / أ ج ر
mots.sens: do you ask them a wage

mots.racine: غ ر م / ث ق ل
mots.sens: that they are weighed down with debt
mots.def: maghram: debt, burden; muthqal: weighed down.

versets.trad: Or do they possess the Mystery, so that they write it down?
versets.commentaire: am ʿindahumu l-ghayb fa-hum yaktubūn (أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الغَيْب فَهُمْ يَكْتُبون): do they possess the Mystery, that they write it down? Qushayrī: “do they have something of the invisible, of which they have made themselves sole masters, that would dispense them from responding?” A rhetorical question: none possesses the ghayb, save God (cf. s. 72, v. 26). The deniers have no secret access to the truth that would justify their refusal. Their obstinacy rests on nothing — neither imposed wage (v. 46), nor hidden knowledge. It is the very emptiness of their position that the questions unveil. One refuses the light with no light of one's own to fall back on.

versets.ar: فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تَكُن كَصَاحِبِ الْحُوتِ إِذْ نَادَىٰ وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ
versets.tr: fa-ṣbir li-ḥukmi rabbika wa-lā takun ka-ṣāḥibi l-ḥūti idh nādā wa-huwa makẓūmun
versets.trad: So patiently await the decree of your Lord, and be not like the man of the fish when he cried out, choked with grief:
versets.commentaire: fa-ṣbir li-ḥukmi rabbik (فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّك): be patient under the judgement of your Lord. Ṣāḥib al-ḥūt — the man of the fish: Jonah, swallowed by the whale for having outrun the decree by leaving his people in a fit of anger (makẓūm, “swallowing his fury”). Qushayrī: “do not hasten, O Muhammad, the chastisement of your people as Jonah hastened, and underwent what he underwent; hold firm under the course of Our decree, do not contradict Our disposition.” The patience asked is not to outrun God, not to grow impatient with the respite granted to the deniers (v. 45). Jonah is the example not to follow: impatience, even in a prophet, leads to the ordeal of the belly of darkness.

mots.racine: ص ب ر / ح ك م
mots.sens: be patient under the decree of your Lord
mots.def: ṣabara: to be patient; ḥukm: decree.

mots.racine: ص ح ب / ح و ت
mots.sens: be not like the man of the fish
mots.def: ṣāḥib al-ḥūt: Jonah, the companion of the whale.

mots.racine: ن د و / ك ظ م
mots.sens: when he cried out, choked with grief
mots.def: makẓūm: stifled with sorrow or swallowed anger.

versets.ar: لَّوْلَا أَن تَدَارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ لَنُبِذَ بِالْعَرَاءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ
versets.tr: lawlā an tadārakahu niʿmatun min rabbihi la-nubidha bi-l-ʿarāʾi wa-huwa madhmūmun
versets.trad: Had a grace from his Lord not reached him, he would have been cast upon the barren shore, disgraced.
versets.commentaire: lawlā an tadārakahu niʿmatun min rabbih (لَوْلا أَنْ تَدارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّه): had a grace from his Lord not overtaken him. Qushayrī: “had God not shown him mercy by His favour, he would have been cast out in the open, blamed.” Grace overtakes (tadāraka) Jonah at the edge of ruin: from the belly of the fish, his invocation and the divine mercy bring him back. It is the luminous reverse of impatience: even the prophet's fault is redeemed by grace. None, though swallowed in darkness, is beyond reach of God's rescue, if he calls upon Him. The grace that overtakes is stronger than the fault that casts down.

mots.ar: لَّوْلَا أَن تَدَارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ
mots.tr: lawlā an tadārakahu niʿmatun min rabbihi
mots.racine: د ر ك / ن ع م
mots.sens: had a grace from his Lord not overtaken him

mots.racine: ن ب ذ / ع ر ي
mots.sens: he would have been cast in the open
mots.def: nabadha: to cast; ʿarāʾ: bare expanse.

versets.trad: But his Lord chose him and placed him among the righteous.
versets.commentaire: fa-jtabāhu rabbuh (فَاجْتَباهُ رَبُّه): his Lord chose him. Qushayrī: “He chose him, elected him, and placed him among the righteous, sending him to a hundred thousand or more.” The completion of Jonah's story: not only overtaken, but chosen, restored among the righteous, and sent back on mission. The fault did not have the last word: divine election raises the one whom impatience had cast down. It is the hope offered to every wayfarer (cf. v. 33): even after the fall, God's regard can raise up and re-elect. Grace does not merely save from shipwreck; it restores to dignity.

mots.racine: ج ب ي / ر ب ب
ج ع ل / ص ل ح
mots.sens: He placed him among the righteous

versets.ar: وَإِن يَكَادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَارِهِمْ لَمَّا سَمِعُوا الذِّكْرَ وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُ لَمَجْنُونٌ
versets.tr: wa-in yakādu lladhīna kafarū la-yuzliqūnaka bi-abṣārihim lammā samiʿū l-dhikra wa-yaqūlūna innahu la-majnūnun
versets.trad: Those who disbelieve almost strike you down with their glares when they hear the Reminder, and they say: “He is surely possessed!”
versets.commentaire: la-yuzliqūnaka bi-abṣārihim (لَيُزْلِقونَكَ بِأَبْصارِهِمْ): they would almost make you slip with their glares. The sūra returns to its starting point: the charge of madness (v. 2). Qushayrī reports a tradition on the “evil eye” of the envious, but concludes on divine protection: “God preserved him.” The glare laden with hatred would strike the Prophet down, but cannot. And they repeat: “he is possessed!” — the slander of the opening. The circle is closed: between the initial accusation and its return, the whole sūra has demonstrated the opposite. The hostile glare, like slander, breaks upon the one God guards.

mots.racine: ك و د / ك ف ر
mots.def: kāda: to be on the point of.

mots.racine: ز ل ق / ب ص ر

mots.sens: and they say: he is possessed

versets.trad: Yet it is nothing but a Reminder for all the worlds!
versets.commentaire: wa-mā huwa illā dhikrun li-l-ʿālamīn (وَما هُوَ إلّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمين): it is nothing but a Reminder for the worlds. A closing verse that definitively overturns the accusation (v. 2, v. 51). What they call the raving of a madman is in truth a universal Reminder — not for one tribe, one age, or one people, but for all the worlds. The sūra that began by defending a man against slander ends by affirming the infinite reach of his Word. The “madman” they mocked bears the Reminder destined for all humanity. The slander is swept away: it is not the madness of one, but the light for all.

mots.sens: it is nothing but a Reminder
